SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering
why: Established the agent-computer interface paradigm behind modern coding-agent scaffolds.

title: OpenHands: An Open Platform for AI Software Developers as Generalist Agents
why: Widely-used open agent platform for software engineering; common baseline scaffold.

title: RefactorBench: Evaluating Stateful Reasoning in Language Agents Through Code
why: Direct analog to the SWE Atlas Refactoring split — repository-level refactor evaluation.

title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Replicate AI Research
why: OpenAI eval of agents reproducing ICML papers from scratch with rubric grading — research-replication frontier.

title: LAB-Bench: Measuring Capabilities of Language Models for Biology Research
why: FutureHouse benchmark of practical biology-research tasks; precedent for expert-curated science evals.

title: LLMs Outperform Experts on Challenging Biology Benchmarks
why: Cautions on biology-benchmark saturation and eval errors — directly motivates SciPredict's calibration framing.

title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
why: The capability predictor MoReBench shows breaks down: bigger isn't better at moral reasoning.

title: The Moral Machine experiment
why: Landmark global study of human moral preferences over machine decisions; foundational moral-ML reference.

title: Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think: Unfaithful Explanations in Chain-of-Thought Prompting
why: CoT can be unfaithful — directly bears on whether graded thinking traces reflect a model's real reasoning.

title: ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs
why: The canonical large-scale tool-learning benchmark; the API-orchestration lineage MCP-Atlas extends to real MCP servers.

title: τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
why: Influential tool-agent-user interaction benchmark (retail/airline) and the basis of τ2-Bench; a key tool-use eval baseline.

title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants
why: Widely-used multi-tool assistant benchmark (web search + code) testing planning, error recovery, and orchestration.

title: ProfBench: Multi-Domain Rubrics requiring Professional Knowledge to Answer and Judge
why: Closest sibling: a multi-domain professional rubric benchmark (2,448 criteria) that PRBench is 'an order of magnitude larger' than.

title: The AI Productivity Index (APEX)
why: A professional rubric benchmark (5,818 criteria over 200 tasks) and a direct Table-4 comparison point for PRBench's scale and methodology.

title: Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
why: The MAST failure taxonomy ResearchRubrics maps its reasoning-action-mismatch and task-disobedience failures onto.

title: Towards an AI co-scientist
why: DeepMind's multi-agent hypothesis-generating co-scientist; the frontier of autonomous research agents ResearchRubrics aims to evaluate.

title: Mind2Web 2: Evaluating Agentic Search with Agent-as-a-Judge
why: Agent-as-a-Judge benchmark for agentic search (50 rubrics/task); a key Table-1 comparison point ResearchRubrics positions against.

title: RE-Bench: Evaluating Frontier AI R&D Capabilities of Language Model Agents against Human Experts
why: METR's head-to-head of agents vs human experts on frontier AI R&D tasks — a human-anchored capability benchmark like RLI.

title: HCAST: Human-Calibrated Autonomy Software Tasks
why: METR's human-time-calibrated autonomy benchmark — directly compared against RLI in Figure 6 and central to the time-horizon framing of agent capability.

title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
why: Foundational realistic web-agent environment; a core computer/web-use benchmark RLI positions its broader remote-work scope against.

title: R3: Robust Rubric-Agnostic Reward Models
why: Trains a single robust reward model that generalizes across domains using diverse rubrics — a rubric-as-reward-model contrast to OnlineRubrics' online augmentation.

title: Agentless: Demystifying LLM-based Software Engineering Agents
why: The influential 'simple beats agentic' SWE pipeline; SWE-Bench Pro tried it as a scaffold and found it weak at multi-file editing.

title: Multi-SWE-bench: A Multilingual Benchmark for Issue Resolving
why: Extends SWE-bench beyond Python to 7 languages (1,632 instances); the multilingual issue-resolution sibling SWE-Bench Pro builds on.

title: SWE-bench Multimodal: Do AI Systems Generalize to Visual Software Domains?
why: Extends SWE-bench to visual/JS software with screenshots and UI bugs — a direct SWE-bench descendant for multimodal coding agents.

title: LiveBench: A Challenging, Contamination-Limited LLM Benchmark
why: Monthly-refreshed, contamination-free benchmark; the contamination-resistance motivation SWE-Bench Pro shares and cites.

title: Beyond Final Answers: Evaluating Large Language Models for Math Tutoring
why: Closest math-tutoring eval — separates problem-solving accuracy from instructional quality and finds only 56.6% of LLM tutoring dialogues fully correct.

title: Training LLM-based Tutors to Improve Student Learning Outcomes in Dialogues
why: The training-side counterpart — RL-tunes LLM tutors to improve student learning outcomes in dialogue, where TutorBench only measures the final response.

title: Generative AI without guardrails can harm learning: Evidence from high school mathematics
why: The PNAS field study TutorBench cites as motivation: high-school students performed worse on exams once the GenAI tutor was taken away.

title: Train Short, Test Long: Attention with Linear Biases Enables Input Length Extrapolation
authors: Ofir Press, Noah A. Smith, Mike Lewis
why: ALiBi — the linear-bias positional method (used by MPT-30B) for length extrapolation; central to the long-context-mechanism question Lost in the Middle raises.

title: When Not to Trust Language Models: Investigating Effectiveness of Parametric and Non-Parametric Memories
why: The parametric-vs-retrieval memory trade-off (when retrieval helps vs hurts) that frames Lost in the Middle's closed-book-vs-context comparison; a RAG-cluster node.

title: Program Synthesis with Large Language Models
why: MBPP — alongside HumanEval, the canonical function-level code-generation benchmark the repo-level SWE-bench line moves beyond.

title: Competition-Level Code Generation with AlphaCode
why: DeepMind's competitive-programming milestone; the contest-style code-generation lineage that precedes real-repository issue resolution.

title: CodeXGLUE: A Machine Learning Benchmark Dataset for Code Understanding and Generation
why: Microsoft's 14-task code-understanding-and-generation suite; the match-based code-eval lineage HumanEval's execution grading was a reaction to.

title: Attention Is All You Need
why: The Transformer — the architecture under every model in the atlas and the single largest citation hub available.

title: Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills
why: A direct IG comparison system and the closest agentic-insight-generation sibling — flat parallel analyst fleet plus hierarchical consolidation.

title: AFlow: Automating Agentic Workflow Generation
why: Highest-cite agent-optimization reference; the automated agent-as-code search lineage IG's insights are meant to feed in a closed loop.

title: Toward Expert-Level Medical Question Answering with Large Language Models
why: Med-PaLM 2 — the expert-level medical-QA follow-up; the direct predecessor in the medical-eval line HealthBench supersedes.

title: Capabilities of GPT-4 on Medical Challenge Problems
why: The GPT-4-on-USMLE evaluation (Nori et al.) — the frontier-model-saturates-medical-exams result that motivated moving past multiple-choice medical benchmarks.

title: WebGPT: Browser-assisted question-answering with human feedback
why: Browser-assisted QA trained with human feedback — a foundational RLHF + retrieval-agent node (HealthBench borrows its worst-at-k / best-of-n U-statistic estimator from its appendix); bridges health to the agents/RL cluster.

title: WildChat: 1M ChatGPT Interaction Logs in the Wild
why: 1M real ChatGPT conversation logs — the in-the-wild interaction data behind realistic conversation distributions (cited for user-style variation); sibling to the WildBench rubric-eval lineage.

title: Spurious Rewards: Rethinking Training Signals in RLVR
why: Shows RLVR gains can come from spurious or even random reward signals rather than newly learned capability — the diagnostic motivation for criterion-level reward inspection that POW3R's pressure analysis extends.

title: DSBench: How Far Are Data Science Agents from Becoming Data Science Experts?
why: A concurrent Kaggle-based data-science-agent benchmark; the template-filtered sibling whose easier, more standardized task selection MLE-bench contrasts with its manually-ported diversity.

title: AI Agents That Matter
why: The cost-controlled critique of agent evaluation — argues benchmarks must report compute/accuracy trade-offs — directly relevant to MLE-bench's pass@k, time, and GPU scaling experiments.

title: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning
why: Turns chains of thought into training signal — generate rationales, keep those that reach the right answer, finetune on them; the bridge from CoT prompting to reasoning RL.

title: Beyond the Imitation Game: Quantifying and Extrapolating the Capabilities of Language Models
why: BIG-bench — 204-task collaborative benchmark probing capabilities beyond existing evals; foundational broad-coverage LLM eval.

title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
why: HELM — Stanford's holistic, multi-metric, multi-scenario evaluation framework; the methodology touchstone for evaluating models across many scenarios and metrics.

title: WebVoyager: Building an End-to-End Web Agent with Large Multimodal Models
why: End-to-end multimodal web agent + benchmark over 15 live websites; the canonical real-website browsing-agent eval, sibling to WebArena.

title: τ2-Bench: Evaluating Conversational Agents in a Dual-Control Environment
why: Dual-control successor to τ-bench (both user and agent act on shared state); the conversational tool-agent eval frontier — direct sibling to τ-bench already in queue.

title: WorkArena: How Capable Are Web Agents at Solving Common Knowledge Work Tasks?
why: Web agents on common enterprise knowledge-work tasks (ServiceNow); a foundational knowledge-work agent benchmark for the remote-work cluster.

title: AppWorld: A Controllable World of Apps and People for Benchmarking Interactive Coding Agents
why: ACL 2024 best paper — a controllable world of 9 apps / 457 APIs for interactive coding agents; a seminal tool-use/agent environment.

title: TheAgentCompany: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Consequential Real World Tasks
why: Simulates a software company so agents perform consequential multi-step knowledge work; a leading real-world-task agent benchmark.

title: Cybench: A Framework for Evaluating Cybersecurity Capabilities and Risks of Language Models
why: Framework for evaluating LLM cybersecurity capabilities and risks (40 CTF tasks); named in frontier-safety frameworks — the cyber-agent counterpart to MLE-bench.

title: RubricHub: A Comprehensive and Highly Discriminative Rubric Dataset via Automated Coarse-to-Fine Generation
why: The rubric dataset every training/eval prompt in this paper is paired with — automated coarse-to-fine rubric generation at scale; the rubric-authoring substrate the whole reward-hacking analysis runs on.

title: AdvancedIF: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and Reinforcement Learning for Advancing LLM Instruction Following
why: The prior rubric-RL work that first observed models generating artifacts and verbose self-evaluations to fool rubric verifiers, and proposed anti-hacking criteria as a countermeasure — the closest sibling to this paper's verifier-failure analysis.

title: Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization
authors: Leo Gao, John Schulman, Jacob Hilton
why: The foundational reward-overoptimization result — proxy reward and gold reward diverge as you optimize a learned reward model; the Goodhart-curve precedent the proxy/reference decomposition in this paper instantiates for rubrics.

title: DR Tulu: Reinforcement Learning with Evolving Rubrics for Deep Research
why: Trains deep-research agents with rubrics that evolve during training — the 'static rubrics go stale as the policy changes' remedy this paper cites; a direct online-rubric counterpart to its offline-rubric failure analysis.

title: Training AI Co-Scientists Using Rubric Rewards
why: Uses rubric rewards to train scientific-reasoning agents and notes self-graded rubric gains may not transfer to stronger evaluators — the transfer-failure concern this paper formalizes with its cross-family reference panel.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
why: The foundational RLHF result — learn a reward model from human preference comparisons, then optimize it — that ETHICS frames value learning against and that the entire post-training/rubric-reward cluster descends from; a major missing hub.

title: Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
why: Anthropic's HH-RLHF — the canonical helpful/harmless preference-data + RLHF recipe and a central safety/alignment hub linking the moral-values line to the post-training cluster.

title: Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models
why: GCG — the automated, transferable adversarial-suffix jailbreak that became the reference attack for aligned LLMs; the foundational red-teaming node the ASPI/jailbreak cluster builds on.

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization: Eliciting Strong Capabilities With Weak Supervision
why: OpenAI's superalignment result — can a weak supervisor elicit a strong model's full capability? — led by an ETHICS co-author; the scalable-oversight node for the CoT-monitorability / weak-to-strong-monitoring cluster.

title: Social Chemistry 101: Learning to Reason about Social and Moral Norms
why: The closest contemporaneous sibling to ETHICS — a corpus of social/moral rules-of-thumb (rules, judgments, and attributes) for reasoning about everyday norms; anchors the moral-reasoning subgraph alongside ETHICS and MoReBench.

title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
why: The default agent scaffold MCP-Universe runs every model in, and the foundational reasoning-and-acting prompting pattern; a major connector hub for the agents cluster.

title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
why: The verbal-reinforcement agent design pattern named alongside ReAct; a high-cite scaffold node for self-reflective tool-use agents.

title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
why: Deliberate multi-path reasoning over a search tree; a major reasoning hub the agent and test-time-compute lineage builds on.

title: MCP-RADAR: A Multi-Dimensional Benchmark for Evaluating Tool Use Capabilities in Large Language Models
why: The contemporary MCP benchmark MCP-Universe contrasts in Table 1 — it adapts HumanEval/GSM8K into MCP wrappers; a direct MCP-eval sibling to MCP-Universe and MCP-Atlas.

title: MCPEval: Automatic MCP-based Deep Evaluation for AI Agent Models
why: Salesforce's own automatic MCP evaluation — the LLM-as-a-judge MCP eval that MCP-Universe positions its execution-based grading against.

title: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
why: Concurrent work isolating and calibrating position bias in LLM-as-judge (balanced position calibration + human-in-the-loop) — the closest sibling to MT-Bench's bias analysis and a foundational LLM-as-judge critique.

title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluations?
why: Among the first to systematically test LLMs as a stand-in for human raters and show they reproduce human judgments — the direct LLM-as-evaluator precedent MT-Bench formalizes and validates.

title: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback
why: Uses LLM judges to cheaply simulate human preference for studying RLHF, and ships AlpacaEval — the automated win-rate eval the rubric-RL cluster (OnlineRubrics, POW3R) reports on; a key LLM-as-judge-for-training node.

title: PandaLM: An Automatic Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Instruction Tuning Optimization
why: Trains a dedicated, reproducible judge model (rather than relying on GPT-4) to compare instruction-tuned LLMs — the fine-tuned-judge alternative to MT-Bench's GPT-4-as-judge.

title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
why: The open foundation model the entire MT-Bench cast (Vicuna, Alpaca, Koala) is fine-tuned from and a top-tier citation hub touching most of the atlas; one of the single largest missing connector nodes.

title: LexGLUE: A Benchmark Dataset for Legal Language Understanding in English
why: The GLUE-for-law benchmark suite (7 legal-NLU tasks) that LegalBench positions itself against; the fine-tuning-era legal-eval predecessor the few-shot LegalBench paradigm moves beyond.

title: CUAD: An Expert-Annotated NLP Dataset for Legal Contract Review
why: The expert-annotated contract-review corpus LegalBench restructures into many of its interpretation/clause-classification tasks; a foundational expert-built legal NLP dataset (and a Hendrycks benchmark linking to the ETHICS lineage).

title: When does pretraining help? Assessing self-supervised learning for law and the CaseHOLD dataset of 53,000+ legal holdings
why: CaseHOLD — the multiple-choice legal-holding benchmark and domain-pretraining study from the same Stanford RegLab group (Zheng, Guha, Ho); the legal-LM pretraining precedent to LegalBench.

title: True Few-Shot Learning with Language Models
why: Shows few-shot model selection is far weaker once you can't tune on held-out data — the prompt/demonstration-sensitivity critique LegalBench's lower-bound prompting design and in-context-demo variance experiments sit within.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
why: The foundational RAG method — pair a parametric LM with a non-parametric retriever; the open-book retrieval paradigm FinanceBench's vector-store configs and the entire RAG-eval cluster are built on. A ~15k-cite hub.

title: FinQA: A Dataset of Numerical Reasoning over Financial Data
why: Expert-written numerical-reasoning-over-financial-data QA (8k+ pairs); the finance-QA precursor FinanceBench extends from curated tables to full open-book filings.

title: ConvFinQA: Exploring the Chain of Numerical Reasoning in Conversational Finance Question Answering
why: Conversational extension of FinQA (3,892 dialogues, 14,115 questions) with chained numerical reasoning; the multi-turn finance-QA sibling FinanceBench's single-turn design contrasts with.

title: FinBen: A Holistic Financial Benchmark for Large Language Models
why: A broad-coverage financial benchmark spanning many tasks/datasets; the holistic successor that aggregates and extends FinanceBench-style finance evals.

title: Judging the Judges: Evaluating Alignment and Vulnerabilities in LLMs-as-Judges
why: Systematic meta-eval of LLM-as-judge alignment and vulnerabilities; ties the finance/rubric evals to the MT-Bench LLM-as-judge cluster and the grading-reliability question FinanceBench's hand-labeling sidesteps.

title: LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations
authors: Arjun Panickssery, Samuel R. Bowman, Shi Feng
why: The self-preference result — LLM judges score their own generations higher — that directly undercuts GDPval's GPT-based automated grader and the whole LLM-as-judge cluster (MT-Bench, rubric evals).

title: Generative AI at Work
authors: Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, Lindsey Raymond
why: Landmark field study of generative-AI productivity at work (customer-support agents +14%); the economic-impact backbone GDPval cites and the RLI / SWE-Lancer / UpBench economic-value cluster sits within.

title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models
why: The labor-market-exposure framework whose GPT-classifies-each-task-as-digital methodology GDPval reuses to pick its occupations; foundational AI-and-jobs reference.

title: Clio: Privacy-Preserving Insights into Real-World AI Use
why: Anthropic's privacy-preserving analysis of real-world AI usage; the usage-pattern lineage GDPval positions its direct capability measure against.

title: How Do AI Agents Do Human Work? Comparing AI and Human Workflows Across Diverse Occupations
why: A GDPval-adjacent study comparing AI versus human workflows across occupations; the closest occupational-workflow sibling in the economic-automation cluster.

title: Measuring short-form factuality in large language models
why: SimpleQA — the same-authors short-form factuality benchmark whose easy-verify single-string-answer methodology BrowseComp directly inverts into hard-to-find questions; its direct parent.

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
why: Foundational tool-use result — LMs self-supervise API calls; a high-cite hub for the tool-augmented / browsing-agent lineage alongside ReAct and WebGPT.

title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-hop Question Answering
why: The canonical multi-hop QA benchmark (~4,700 cites); the easy-to-find retrieval lineage BrowseComp explicitly moves past and a major eval hub.

title: WebShop: Towards Scalable Real-World Web Interaction with Grounded Language Agents
why: Shunyu Yao's grounded web-interaction environment; an early, foundational web-agent benchmark in the lineage that runs through WebArena/WebVoyager to modern browsing agents.

title: TriviaQA: A Large Scale Distantly Supervised Challenge Dataset for Reading Comprehension
why: Classic distantly-supervised reading-comprehension/retrieval QA (~3,900 cites); part of the easy-retrieval benchmark family BrowseComp contrasts itself against.

title: WebThinker: Empowering Large Reasoning Models with Deep Research Capability
why: A deep-research framework that gives large reasoning models autonomous search-and-synthesis; the highest-cited DRA system DeepResearch Bench evaluates against.

title: DeepResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Reinforcement Learning in Real-world Environments
why: Trains a Deep Research Agent end-to-end with RL in live web environments — the training-side counterpart to DeepResearch Bench's evaluation-side framing.

title: Assisting in Writing Wikipedia-like Articles From Scratch with Large Language Models
why: STORM — the grounded long-form report writer and static-rubric report-generation baseline RACE's adaptive criteria are built to move past.

title: WebWalker: Benchmarking LLMs in Web Traversal
why: A multi-step web-traversal QA benchmark; the short-verifiable-answer DRA eval style DeepResearch Bench argues fails to capture long-form synthesis.

title: LongWriter: Unleashing 10,000+ Word Generation from Long Context LLMs
why: 10,000+ word generation with fixed-checklist scoring — the long-form-generation eval lineage RACE's dynamic, reference-based grading contrasts with.

title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Software Tasks
why: METR's time-horizon benchmark — the '50%-completion time horizon' doubling roughly every 7 months; the human-time-calibrated capability axis the RLI / HCAST / SWE-Lancer economic cluster is anchored to.

title: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developers
why: METR's randomized controlled trial finding experienced OSS developers were ~19% slower with early-2025 AI tools — the field-study counterweight to SWE-Lancer's automation-potential framing.

title: Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? A Large-Scale Human Study with 100+ NLP Researchers
why: Si et al.'s large-scale human study on LLM research-idea generation — the proposal/idea-evaluation precedent SWE-Lancer explicitly cites for its SWE Manager (proposal-selection) tasks.

title: Establishing Best Practices for Building Rigorous Agentic Benchmarks
why: A methodology audit of agentic benchmarks for task-validity and grading rigor — directly on-theme for the atlas's eval-rigor lens and a checklist SWE-Lancer-style E2E grading is measured against.

title: Kimi K2: Open Agentic Intelligence
why: Moonshot's open agentic MoE model reporting SWE-Lancer scores; a high-cite open-weight frontier-agent node for the agents cluster.

title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
why: The canonical zero-shot IR benchmark suite (18 datasets) whose query sets Semantic Stratification diagnoses; the foundational retrieval-eval node anchoring the new RAG cluster.

title: From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization
why: GraphRAG — the closest methodological sibling: also Leiden-clusters an LLM-built entity graph, but for retrieval optimization rather than benchmark diagnosis.

title: Natural Questions: A Benchmark for Question Answering Research
why: Foundational open-domain QA benchmark from real Google queries; a cornerstone of the retrieval/QA lineage BEIR aggregates.

title: MS MARCO: A Human Generated MAchine Reading COmprehension Dataset
why: The large-scale, real-query MRC/passage-ranking dataset that became the backbone of neural IR training and evaluation.

title: Navigating the Digital World as Humans Do: Universal Visual Grounding for GUI Agents
why: UGround — universal pixel-level visual grounding for GUI agents, attacking the exact mouse-click/grounding failure OSWorld pinpoints as the wall; the highest-cited grounding successor.

title: OS-ATLAS: A Foundation Action Model for Generalist GUI Agents
why: A foundation action model trained on a 13M-element cross-platform GUI corpus and evaluated on OSWorld; the open grounding-model backbone the computer-use wave standardized on.

title: AndroidWorld: A Dynamic Benchmarking Environment for Autonomous Agents
why: Google's execution-graded mobile counterpart to OSWorld — 116 dynamically-parameterized tasks across 20 real Android apps; the mobile-OS sibling in the computer-use cluster.

title: Aguvis: Unified Pure Vision Agents for Autonomous GUI Interaction
why: A pure-vision (screenshot-only) GUI agent — the configuration OSWorld argues is the long-run ideal — sharing an OSWorld co-author (Yiheng Xu); a leading screenshot-native successor.

title: Windows Agent Arena: Evaluating Multi-Modal OS Agents at Scale
why: Microsoft's Windows-native OS-agent benchmark built directly on the OSWorld framework (154 tasks, parallelized in Azure); the direct Windows extension of OSWorld's environment.

title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
why: The foundational 'AI feedback against written principles' work — the conceptual ancestor of criteria/rubric-as-reward that Rubric Anchors, Checklists, and Rule-Based Rewards all descend from.

title: DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report
why: The 671B open MoE model Rubicon benchmarks against and beats by +2.4% on humanities with a 30B model; the base behind DeepSeek-R1 and a major hub node.

title: TÜLU 3: Pushing Frontiers in Open Language Model Post-Training
why: Allen AI's fully-open post-training recipe that named and popularized RLVR — the framework Rubric Anchors extends to non-verifiable domains; a central post-training hub node.

title: Rule Based Rewards for Language Model Safety
why: OpenAI's rules-as-reward method — decomposing safety into explicit LLM-scored propositions; the closest sibling to rubric-as-reward, applied to safety.

title: WritingBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Generative Writing
why: The open-ended generative-writing benchmark (query-dependent criteria) Rubicon uses as a primary eval and gains +4.5 on; a writing-eval node for the open-ended cluster.

title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
why: DPO — the reference-free preference-optimization algorithm RLCF, OnlineRubrics, and most of the rubric-RL cluster train with; one of the single largest missing connector hubs for the post-training cluster.

title: Instruction-Following Evaluation for Large Language Models
why: IFEval — the standard programmatically-verifiable instruction-following benchmark RLCF and nearly every alignment paper report on; the foundational instruction-following eval node missing from the graph.

title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling
why: The reward-model evaluation benchmark RLCF uses (Table 5) to show top RMs (Skywork, ArmoRM) win at static preference accuracy yet teach RL worse — central to the 'RM accuracy ≠ RL efficacy' thesis and a hub for the reward-model cluster.

title: TICKing All the Boxes: Generated Checklists Improve LLM Evaluation and Generation
why: The closest predecessor — model-generated checklists used at inference time for evaluation and generation; RLCF's direct conceptual parent, brought from inference-time grading into RL training.

title: Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms
why: PPO — the actor-critic policy-gradient algorithm GRPO is a variant of; the foundation under RLHF and every RL fine-tuning method in the atlas, and one of the largest available connector hubs.

title: Let's Verify Step by Step
why: OpenAI's PRM800K / process reward models — the process-supervision lineage GRPO's process-supervised variant builds on, and the '~20% mislabeled' caution DeepSeekMath cites on noisy reward signals.

title: High-Dimensional Continuous Control Using Generalized Advantage Estimation
why: GAE — the advantage estimator PPO relies on and GRPO replaces with a group-normalized baseline; completes the GAE→PPO→GRPO RL lineage.

title: Solving Quantitative Reasoning Problems with Language Models
why: Minerva — the closed 540B math model DeepSeekMath-Base 7B matches; the prior math-reasoning frontier and DeepSeekMath's central capability comparison.

title: Math-Shepherd: Verify and Reinforce LLMs Step-by-step without Human Annotations
why: Process reward models trained without human step labels; the process-supervision recipe DeepSeekMath follows for GRPO+PS, and a sibling in the verifier/reward cluster.

title: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models
why: FLAN — the foundational instruction-finetuning recipe (scaling tasks + CoT data) behind the SFT half of the conventional SFT→RL post-training paradigm DeepSeek-R1 contrasts its pure-RL approach against; a major post-training hub.

title: MMLU-Pro: A More Robust and Challenging Multi-Task Language Understanding Benchmark
why: The harder, reasoning-focused successor to MMLU (10 options, tougher distractors) that R1 and most frontier models now report; the benchmark hub the knowledge-eval cluster has migrated to.

title: DROP: A Reading Comprehension Benchmark Requiring Discrete Reasoning Over Paragraphs
why: The classic discrete-reasoning-over-text benchmark (counting, sorting, arithmetic over passages) R1 reports (3-shot F1 92.2); a pre-LLM reasoning-eval node bridging reading comprehension to the math-reasoning line.

title: From Crowdsourced Data to High-Quality Benchmarks: Arena-Hard and BenchBuilder Pipeline
why: Arena-Hard — the automatic LLM-as-judge benchmark distilled from Chatbot Arena prompts that R1, OnlineRubrics, Rubicon and the rubric-RL cluster all report on; the LMSYS-lineage descendant of MT-Bench and a high-connectivity eval node.

title: C-Eval: A Multi-Level Multi-Discipline Chinese Evaluation Suite for Foundation Models
why: The canonical Chinese-language knowledge benchmark (52 disciplines) R1 reports (91.8 EM); the Chinese counterpart to MMLU and an anchor for the bilingual DeepSeek/Qwen model line.

title: DeepSeek-Coder: When the Large Language Model Meets Programming - The Rise of Code Intelligence
why: The widely-used open code-foundation model line (1.3B–33B, repo-level pretraining) behind many BigCodeBench/SWE-bench baselines; the DeepSeek code lineage feeding DeepSeekMath/R1.

title: Gorilla: Large Language Model Connected with Massive APIs
why: The API/function-calling benchmark whose 'code function call as tool' framing (Gorilla OpenFunctions) BigCodeBench adopts; an early node in the tool-use lineage of ToolLLM, MCP-Universe, and MCP-Atlas.

title: CodeGen: An Open Large Language Model for Code with Multi-Turn Program Synthesis
why: A foundational open code model that introduced multi-turn program synthesis (the MTPB benchmark); part of the pre-Codex-era open code-LLM lineage HumanEval-style benchmarks evaluated.

title: DS-1000: A Natural and Reliable Benchmark for Data Science Code Generation
why: The reliable, perturbation-hardened data-science code-gen benchmark (1,000 StackOverflow tasks, 7 libraries) BigCodeBench directly contrasts itself against in Table 1 — the closest function-level, library-oriented sibling.

title: Teaching Large Language Models to Self-Debug
why: Introduces self-debugging via execution feedback and explanation; the test-driven code-repair technique behind coding-agent scaffolds (Agentless, SWE-agent) and the execution-feedback loop BigCodeBench's construction reused.

title: Can LLM Already Serve as A Database Interface? A BIg bench for large-scale database grounded text-to-SQLs (BIRD)
why: BIRD — the large-scale, database-grounded text-to-SQL benchmark HiL-Bench draws all 150 of its SQL tasks from; the canonical real-world SQL eval and a major missing hub.

title: QuestBench: Can LLMs ask the right question to acquire information in reasoning tasks?
why: The closest clarification-benchmark sibling — formalizes whether LLMs can ask the single right question to fill an information gap in constraint-satisfaction reasoning; HiL-Bench's upfront-ambiguity counterpart it extends with progressive discovery.

title: Teaching Language Models To Gather Information Proactively
why: Named the closest prior work — constructs tasks with multiple implicit blockers and applies RL to improve downstream performance; HiL-Bench's direct predecessor, which it critiques for using upfront synthetic ambiguities without progressive discovery or a spam-penalizing metric.

title: Learning to Ask: When LLM Agents Meet Unclear Instruction
why: Evaluates tool-use agents under unclear instructions — the agentic-clarification precedent HiL-Bench builds on, but without independent multi-blocker tasks surfacing through execution.

title: STaR-GATE: Teaching Language Models to Ask Clarifying Questions
why: Self-improvement (STaR-style) training that teaches LMs to generate clarifying questions to elicit user preferences; the train-to-ask precedent HiL-Bench's shaped-Ask-F1 RLVR result extends to selective escalation.

title: OR-Bench: An Over-Refusal Benchmark for Large Language Models
why: The canonical over-refusal benchmark (80k prompts) — the direct measurement sibling to Defensive Refusal Bias, generalizing 'safety guardrails reject benign requests' beyond the cyber-defense setting.

title: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal
why: The standardized red-teaming/robust-refusal benchmark that defines the 'harmful compliance' axis Defensive Refusal Bias argues must be balanced against false refusals; high-cite safety hub (shares author with RLI).

title: Jailbroken: How Does LLM Safety Training Fail?
why: The foundational analysis of why safety training fails (competing objectives, mismatched generalization) — the jailbreak side of the same alignment tradeoff Defensive Refusal Bias studies from the over-refusal side.

title: CYBERSECEVAL 3: Advancing the Evaluation of Cybersecurity Risks and Capabilities in Large Language Models
why: Meta's cyber-capability/risk eval suite whose 'False Refusal Rate' on synthetic CTF challenges is the closest prior cyber-refusal measurement Defensive Refusal Bias moves into a real, sanctioned competition.

title: MathDial: A Dialogue Tutoring Dataset with Rich Pedagogical Properties Grounded in Math Reasoning Problems
why: One of the two source datasets MRBench is built from — multi-turn math tutoring dialogues with rich pedagogical annotations; the core tutoring-dialogue corpus behind the MRBench/TutorBench cluster (and the source of the 'ChatGPT reveals the answer 66% of the time' finding).

title: Bridging the Novice-Expert Gap via Models of Decision-Making: A Case Study on Remediating Math Mistakes
why: The Bridge dataset — paired novice/expert human tutor responses to student math mistakes; MRBench's other source corpus and the novice-vs-expert pedagogical contrast it grades against.

title: Prometheus 2: An Open Source Language Model Specialized in Evaluating Other Language Models
why: The open specialized LLM-as-judge MRBench tests as an automatic evaluator (and finds correlates negatively with human pedagogy labels); a high-cite LLM-as-judge node connecting to the MT-Bench / rubric-eval cluster.

title: The AI Teacher Test: Measuring the Pedagogical Ability of Blender and GPT-3 in Educational Dialogues
why: The foundational pedagogical-ability evaluation (speaks-like-a-teacher / understands / helps) that MRBench's unified taxonomy subsumes — the earliest of the four prior schemata it reconciles.

title: Towards Responsible Development of Generative AI for Education: An Evaluation-Driven Approach
why: Google's large-scale, evaluation-driven study of generative-AI tutoring that called for well-recognized unified metrics — the direct motivation MRBench's taxonomy answers; bridges the tutoring cluster to frontier-lab education work (LearnLM).

title: MMMU: A Massive Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark for Expert AGI
why: The canonical massive multi-discipline multimodal benchmark (11.5K college-level questions, 30 subjects); the broad-coverage multimodal-eval hub OlympiadBench positions its harder, deeper math/physics focus against.

title: MathVista: Evaluating Mathematical Reasoning of Foundation Models in Visual Contexts
why: The multimodal mathematical-reasoning benchmark OlympiadBench directly contrasts with — aggregates 31 datasets but, per the authors, doesn't delve into the problem complexity olympiad problems demand.

title: Learn to Explain: Multimodal Reasoning via Thought Chains for Science Question Answering
why: ScienceQA — the foundational multimodal science-QA benchmark with chain-of-thought rationales (21K questions); an anchor of the multimodal-reasoning lineage OlympiadBench escalates in difficulty.

title: Sparks of Artificial General Intelligence: Early experiments with GPT-4
why: Microsoft's widely-cited early-capabilities study of GPT-4 — the 'approaching human-expert level' framing that motivates harder benchmarks like OlympiadBench; a high-connectivity hub node.

title: TheoremQA: A Theorem-driven Question Answering dataset
why: The first theorem-driven QA dataset (800 expert questions over 350 theorems across math/physics/EE&CS/finance); the proof-and-theorem eval sibling to OlympiadBench's 21% theorem-proving slice.

title: Search Arena: Analyzing Search-Augmented LLMs
why: A large-scale crowd-sourced analysis of search-augmented LLMs — the info-seeking eval lineage STC recommends prioritizing over offline capability benchmarks.

title: BEARCUBS: A benchmark for computer-using web agents
why: A live-web benchmark for computer-using agents designed so answers can't be memorized — the contamination-resistant, search-native eval STC argues for.

title: Towards Reliable Benchmarking: A Contamination Free, Controllable Evaluation
why: A direct follow-on to STC's thesis — builds a contamination-free, controllable evaluation framework for the exact leakage problem STC identifies.

title: Language Models are Multilingual Chain-of-Thought Reasoners
why: MGSM — the canonical translated multilingual reasoning benchmark (250 GSM8K problems × 10 languages) whose translate-from-English approach MultiNRC defines itself against.

title: MultiLoKo: a multilingual local knowledge benchmark for LLMs spanning 31 languages
why: The local-knowledge multilingual benchmark MultiNRC borrows its 'Mother Tongue Effect' (original − English) framing from; closest sibling on native-vs-English comparison.

title: MMLU-ProX: A Multilingual Benchmark for Advanced Large Language Model Evaluation
why: MMLU-Pro translated and expert-reviewed across 28 languages — the translation-based multilingual reasoning eval whose English-centric framing MultiNRC critiques.

title: M3Exam: A Multilingual, Multimodal, Multilevel Benchmark for Examining Large Language Models
why: The native (non-translated) multilingual exam benchmark MultiNRC contrasts itself against — multiple-choice knowledge recall that is too easy for SOTA models rather than genuine reasoning.

title: BenchMAX: A Comprehensive Multilingual Evaluation Suite for Large Language Models
why: A multilingual suite built by machine-translating GPQA with three-annotator post-editing — the meticulous-translation pipeline MultiNRC positions native authoring against.

title: General-Reasoner: Advancing LLM Reasoning Across All Domains
why: RaR's central 'RLVR beyond STEM' comparison — trains on a 200k mixed-domain corpus and reports a ~10pt MMLU-Pro gain; the cross-domain verifiable-reward line RaR positions rubric rewards against.

title: Process Reward Models That Think
why: ThinkPRM — a generative process reward model that verbalizes step-level verdicts; the dense-supervision alternative (process rewards) RaR cites as the costly precedent its rubric scores approximate.

title: Med-RLVR: Emerging Medical Reasoning from a 3B Base Model via Reinforcement Learning
why: Applies RLVR to multiple-choice clinical QA, eliciting chain-of-thought from a 3B base — the verifiable-reward counterpart in medicine to RaR-Medicine's rubric-reward approach.

title: ExpertLongBench: Benchmarking Language Models on Expert-Level Long-Form Generation Tasks with Structured Checklists
why: Checklist-graded expert long-form generation — the rubric-as-evaluation sibling (alongside PRBench/ResearchRubrics) that RaR turns from a grader into a training reward.

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
why: BERT — the masked-LM pre-training paradigm T5's encoder-decoder span-denoising objective is a direct response to; the single largest missing pre-LLM hub and T5's main point of comparison.

title: GLUE: A Multi-Task Benchmark and Analysis Platform for Natural Language Understanding
why: The 9-task NLU meta-benchmark T5 reports its 90.3 average on; the canonical pre-LLM evaluation hub that aggregate GLUE-style scoring descends from.

title: SuperGLUE: A Stickier Benchmark for General-Purpose Language Understanding Systems
why: The harder GLUE successor built to be difficult for transfer-learning models; T5-11B's 88.9 nearly matched its 89.8 human baseline — the near-saturation result the modern eval wave reacted to.

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
why: The robustly-optimized BERT that held prior SOTA on several GLUE/SuperGLUE tasks T5 compares against; the canonical 'just train BERT longer on more data' encoder-only baseline of the era.

title: ALBERT: A Lite BERT for Self-supervised Learning of Language Representations
why: The parameter-sharing BERT variant holding the previous-best GLUE/SQuAD numbers T5-11B overtook (via 6–17-model ensembles); T5's main 2019 leaderboard rival.

title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
why: DPR — the supervised dense bi-encoder retriever that defined the paradigm and serves as Contriever's supervised ceiling baseline; the foundational retrieval hub the cluster (BEIR, MS MARCO, Lost in the Middle) is anchored on.

title: Momentum Contrast for Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning
why: MoCo — the momentum-encoder + negative-queue framework Contriever borrows wholesale to scale negatives without huge batches; a ~15k-cite contrastive-learning hub bridging retrieval to the CV self-supervision lineage.

title: A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations
why: SimCLR — the canonical contrastive-learning recipe (augmentation + InfoNCE + large negatives) that the whole self-supervised contrastive line, including Contriever's cropping objective, descends from; ~25k cites, one of the largest available hubs.

title: SimCSE: Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings
why: SimCSE — the sentence-embedding contrastive method (dropout-as-augmentation) directly compared as a Figure-1 unsupervised baseline; Contriever's closest NLP sibling in contrastive representation learning.

title: REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training
why: REALM — jointly pretrains a retriever inside an LM via masked-LM signal; a Figure-1 baseline for Contriever and the retrieval-augmented-pretraining precedent bridging dense retrieval to the RAG cluster (Lewis et al.).

title: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
why: GPT-2 — the foundational autoregressive LM FlashAttention benchmarks its 3× training speedup on; a top-tier citation hub and the model behind its longer-context experiments.

title: Megatron-LM: Training Multi-Billion Parameter Language Models Using Model Parallelism
why: The tensor/model-parallelism training system and the optimized GPT-2 baseline FlashAttention beats by ~1.7×; foundational large-model training infrastructure.

title: Long Range Arena: A Benchmark for Efficient Transformers
why: The LRA benchmark (incl. Path-X / Path-256) FlashAttention is evaluated on; the canonical long-context efficiency eval suite anchoring the long-sequence cluster.

title: Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces
authors: Albert Gu, Karan Goel, Christopher Ré
why: S4 — the structured state-space model that first cracked Path-X; the non-attention long-sequence architecture (and Mamba's ancestor) FlashAttention's longer-context Transformers rival.

title: Longformer: The Long-Document Transformer
authors: Iz Beltagy, Matthew E. Peters, Arman Cohan
why: The sliding-window sparse-attention long-document Transformer FlashAttention borrows its positional-embedding-repeat trick from; a canonical efficient-attention/long-context baseline.

title: Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models
why: The 2T-token foundation model every Code Llama variant is specialized from, and the source of its RLHF-V5 instruction/safety data — a central base-model hub across the atlas.

title: StarCoder: May the Source Be with You!
why: The leading code-only open model (15.5B, The Stack) Code Llama benchmarks against; the from-scratch alternative its specialize-from-Llama-2 thesis contrasts with.

title: InCoder: A Generative Model for Code Infilling and Synthesis
why: Introduced the causal-masking infilling objective Code Llama adopts for fill-in-the-middle; the source of the HumanEval-infilling benchmark.

title: Efficient Training of Language Models to Fill in the Middle
why: The FIM recipe (PSM/SPM ordering, the 'FIM-for-free' finding) Code Llama follows for its infilling training.

title: MultiPL-E: A Scalable and Polyglot Approach to Benchmarking Neural Code Generation
why: The multilingual (C++/Java/PHP/TS/C#/Bash) HumanEval+MBPP translation Code Llama claims SOTA on; the polyglot code-eval node extending HumanEval beyond Python.

title: GPT-4 Technical Report
why: The frontier model that tops EvalPlus's leaderboard (88.4→76.2 pass@1 on HumanEval+); a massive citation hub linking nearly every modern eval and agent node in the atlas.

title: Mistral 7B
why: A widely-used open base model evaluated in EvalPlus; a high-citation connector across the open-LLM cluster.

title: WizardCoder: Empowering Code Large Language Models with Evol-Instruct
why: The Evol-Instruct code model central to EvalPlus's mis-ranking finding — it overtakes ChatGPT only once HumanEval+ hardens the tests.

title: CodeGeeX: A Pre-Trained Model for Code Generation with Multilingual Benchmarking on HumanEval-X
why: Introduced HumanEval-X, the multilingual extension of HumanEval — a code-gen benchmark in the same test-adequacy line EvalPlus hardens.

title: A Systematic Evaluation of Large Language Models of Code
why: A systematic evaluation of code LLMs (and the PolyCoder model) — a direct predecessor in the rigorous code-eval line EvalPlus extends.

title: The Pile: An 800GB Dataset of Diverse Text for Language Modeling
why: The 800GB pretraining corpus (incl. GitHub) behind GPT-Neo, APPS's strongest model — a foundational data-lineage hub for the open-model cluster.

title: SPoC: Search-based Pseudocode to Code
why: The pseudocode-to-code benchmark (seq2seq + search) APPS contrasts its unrestricted natural-language-spec, general-purpose-code setting against.

title: Unsupervised Translation of Programming Languages
authors: Marie-Anne Lachaux, Baptiste Rozière, Lowik Chanussot, Guillaume Lample
why: The unsupervised code-translation method (TransCoder) APPS positions its natural-language→code task against; same lead author (Rozière) as Code Llama.

title: Mind2Web: Towards a Generalist Agent for the Web
why: The foundational generalist web-agent benchmark (same OSU group); the real-website action-space lineage WebGuard layers a guardrail on top of.

title: Llama Guard: LLM-based Input-Output Safeguard for Human-AI Conversations
why: The canonical LLM guardrail (conversation-level input/output moderation) WebGuard contrasts with action-level web-agent risk.

title: CogAgent: A Visual Language Model for GUI Agents
why: A foundational VLM backbone for GUI/web agents; the visual-grounding lineage behind the multimodal guardrails WebGuard fine-tunes.

title: VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks
why: Realistic visual web-task benchmark; a core multimodal web-agent eval the WebGuard / WebArena cluster connects to.

title: SeeClick: Harnessing GUI Grounding for Advanced Visual GUI Agents
why: Establishes GUI grounding (predicting click targets) for visual agents — the bounding-box action grounding WebGuards multimodal setting relies on.

title: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training
why: GPT-1 — the original generative-pretraining decoder Transformer GPT-2 and GPT-3 scale up; the direct ancestor of the GPT lineage.

title: Deep Contextualized Word Representations
why: ELMo — the deep contextualized embeddings that opened the pretraining era GPT-3 contrasts its task-agnostic in-context approach against; a foundational representation-learning hub.

title: SQuAD: 100,000+ Questions for Machine Comprehension of Text
why: SQuAD — the canonical reading-comprehension benchmark and a cornerstone of the QA-eval lineage GPT-3 and nearly every later LLM report on.

title: HellaSwag: Can a Machine Really Finish Your Sentence?
why: HellaSwag — the adversarially-filtered commonsense-NLI benchmark GPT-3 reports (78.9% zero-shot); a standard frontier-model commonsense eval.

title: WinoGrande: An Adversarial Winograd Schema Challenge at Scale
why: WinoGrande — the large-scale adversarial Winograd-schema commonsense-coreference benchmark GPT-3 evaluates on; a standard commonsense-reasoning eval.

title: Monitoring Reasoning Models for Misbehavior and the Risks of Promoting Obfuscation
why: The empirical backbone of CoT monitorability: a monitor catches reward hacking, and optimizing against the monitor teaches models to obfuscate their CoT.

title: Alignment faking in large language models
why: Landmark Anthropic/Redwood demonstration that models strategically fake alignment and reveal it in their CoT — the early-misalignment signal monitoring aims to catch.

title: Frontier Models are Capable of In-context Scheming
why: Apollo Research evidence that frontier models scheme in-context and often verbalize it — the misbehavior CoT monitors are meant to flag.

title: When Chain of Thought is Necessary, Language Models Struggle to Evade Monitors
why: Directly tests the necessity argument: when a task forces reasoning into the CoT, models can't hide it from monitors — the closest empirical companion to the position paper.

title: AI Control: Improving Safety Despite Intentional Subversion
why: The foundational AI-control framing (red-team an untrusted model with trusted monitors/resampling) that CoT monitoring slots into as one oversight protocol.

title: Fine-Tuning Language Models from Human Preferences
why: The direct predecessor (7 shared authors) — the first RLHF-for-LM pipeline (reward model from preferences + PPO) that this paper refines with the offline/batch setting and high labeler agreement; a foundational post-training hub.

title: TL;DR: Mining Reddit to Learn Automatic Summarization
venue: EMNLP Workshop on New Frontiers in Summarization
why: The Reddit TL;DR summarization dataset this paper is built on — the source of its ~3M posts and the filtered 123k-post training set.

title: Better Rewards Yield Better Summaries: Learning to Summarise Without References
why: The closest prior summarization-RLHF — learns a reward from human ratings of CNN/DM summaries and beats a ROUGE-optimizing policy; the work this paper most directly extends to larger models and the batch setting.

title: SheetCopilot: Bringing Software Productivity to the Next Level through Large Language Models
why: The canonical spreadsheet agent and SpreadsheetBench's primary product baseline (SheetCopilotBench); anchors the new spreadsheet-manipulation cluster.

title: InstructExcel: A Benchmark for Natural Language Instruction in Excel
why: The prior real-annotation Excel benchmark SpreadsheetBench compares against most directly in Table 1 (4,850 instructions but only 9.8 words each, single test case).

title: SheetAgent: A Generalist Agent for Spreadsheet Reasoning and Manipulation via Large Language Models
why: The generalist spreadsheet agent (and its SheetRM benchmark) SpreadsheetBench critiques as synthetic and oversimplified; a direct spreadsheet-agent predecessor.

title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions
why: The self-instruct synthetic-instruction method whose limitation (queries unlike real user demands) motivates SpreadsheetBench's real-forum sourcing; a high-cite connector hub.

title: SpreadsheetCoder: Formula Prediction from Semi-structured Context
why: The deep-learning formula-prediction predecessor in SpreadsheetBench's intro lineage; foundational pre-LLM spreadsheet-manipulation method.

title: Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents
why: Uses agentic systems to evaluate other agents step-by-step (with the DevAI benchmark) — the agent-as-evaluator approach directly adjacent to TRAIL's structured-trace error localization.

title: Training Software Engineering Agents and Verifiers with SWE-Gym
why: The first training environment for SWE agents and verifiers (built on SWE-Bench); the CodeAct/SWE-agent lineage TRAIL generates its software-engineering traces from.

title: LongBench v2: Towards Deeper Understanding and Reasoning on Realistic Long-context Multitasks
why: The realistic long-context benchmark TRAIL cross-checks its model rankings against; underpins TRAIL's finding that trace debugging is bottlenecked by context length.

title: ACPBench: Reasoning about Action, Change, and Planning
why: A synthetic benchmark for atomic reasoning about action, change, and planning — the planning-only taxonomy TRAIL contrasts itself against by adding execution-level failure categories.

title: DebugBench: Evaluating Debugging Capability of Large Language Models
why: Tests whether LLMs can localize and fix injected bugs given error context — the software-debugging analogue to TRAIL's agent-trace debugging task.

title: SWE-smith: Scaling Data for Software Engineering Agents
why: Scales synthetic training data for SWE agents from the SWE-bench/SWE-agent team (Yang, Jimenez, Press, Schmidt); the data-generation counterpart adapted into Terminal-Bench's harness.

title: CORE-Bench: Fostering the Credibility of Published Research Through a Computational Reproducibility Agent Benchmark
why: A computational-reproducibility agent benchmark (reproduce a paper's results from its code) adapted into Terminal-Bench; bridges to the science-replication cluster (MLE-bench, PaperBench, SciPredict).

title: MLGym: A New Framework and Benchmark for Advancing AI Research Agents
why: Meta's Gym-style framework + benchmark for ML-research agents (hypothesis to training to analysis); the direct sibling to MLE-bench whose failure taxonomy Terminal-Bench's error analysis draws on.

title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
why: The 540B base model under Flan-PaLM/Med-PaLM and a major citation hub across the atlas; foundational dense decoder-only LLM trained with Pathways.

title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
why: Chinchilla — the compute-optimal scaling correction (train smaller models on more tokens); a foundational scaling-law hub across the model-training cluster.

title: The Power of Scale for Parameter-Efficient Prompt Tuning
authors: Brian Lester, Rami Al-Rfou, Noah Constant
why: Introduced soft-prompt tuning — the parameter-efficient method Med-PaLM's instruction prompt tuning extends; the PEFT lineage anchor.

title: Galactica: A Large Language Model for Science
why: LLM trained on scientific corpora and a direct MedQA/MedMCQA baseline Flan-PaLM beats; bridges to the science-eval cluster.

title: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners
why: FLAN — the instruction-tuning origin that Flan-PaLM scales; a foundational post-training hub.

title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction
why: GenRM — recasts reward modeling as next-token prediction with synthesized CoT; the generative-reward-model lineage Rubric-RM's generate-then-judge design extends.

title: RM-R1: Reward Modeling as Reasoning
why: The primary same-size reasoning-judge baseline Rubric-RM is benchmarked against in every table (RM-R1-7B/14B).

title: RewardBench 2: Advancing Reward Model Evaluation
why: The contamination-resistant successor to RewardBench (Precise-IF, Focus) used as a core reward-model eval in this paper; anchors the reward-model-benchmark cluster.

title: Fine-Grained Human Feedback Gives Better Rewards for Language Model Training
why: FG-RLHF — segment-level, multi-dimensional rewards; the conceptual ancestor of decomposing reward into per-criterion signals that rubrics-as-rewards formalizes.

title: Magpie: Alignment Data Synthesis from Scratch by Prompting Aligned LLMs with Nothing
why: A core OpenRubrics data source and a widely-used synthetic-alignment-data method (self-prompting aligned LLMs); a strong connector in the post-training cluster.

title: Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think
why: The Anthropic reward-hacking environment VFT builds on directly — frontier reasoning models exploit prompt cues during RL while rarely verbalizing it; the empirical basis for the verbalization line.

title: Bias-Augmented Consistency Training Reduces Biased Reasoning in Chain-of-Thought
why: BCT — the debiasing baseline VFT beats; trains models to ignore prompt cues, which VFT shows backfires by also suppressing verbalization of hacks.

title: Sycophancy to Subterfuge: Investigating Reward-Tampering in Large Language Models
why: Shows specification gaming can generalize up to direct reward-tampering — the escalation result behind the reward-hacking threat model VFT operates in.

title: Qwen2.5 Technical Report
why: The Qwen2.5-32B base model DAPO trains into a 50-on-AIME reasoner; the open-weight family underpinning most of the post-R1 RLVR reproduction work and a top-tier citation hub.

title: HybridFlow: A Flexible and Efficient RLHF Framework
why: The verl framework — the open RLHF/RL infrastructure DAPO's entire training system is built on; the practical substrate the open-RL reproduction wave standardized on.

title: Kimi k1.5: Scaling Reinforcement Learning with LLMs
why: Moonshot's large-scale RL-for-reasoning report released alongside R1 — long-context RL, length penalties, and policy-optimization recipes; a major sibling in the hidden-recipe cluster DAPO opens up.

title: Open-Reasoner-Zero: An Open Source Approach to Scaling Up Reinforcement Learning on the Base Model
why: The closest open-RL-on-base sibling to DAPO — minimal PPO recipe scaling RL directly on a base model; a direct reproduction-wave counterpart contemporaneous with DAPO.

title: Process Reinforcement through Implicit Rewards
why: PRIME — online process reinforcement with implicit token-level rewards; the process-supervision counterpoint to DAPO's outcome-only ±1 reward, and a prominent RLVR-for-reasoning recipe.

title: Large Language Monkeys: Scaling Inference Compute with Repeated Sampling
why: The repeated-sampling / pass@k inference-scaling result this paper's entire reasoning-boundary metric (and its unbiased estimator) is built on.

title: Understanding R1-Zero-Like Training: A Critical Perspective
why: The Oat-Zero critical study of R1-zero training (the 'no aha moment' result) whose released models this paper evaluates; a high-cite RLVR-analysis sibling.

title: SimpleRL-Zoo: Investigating and Taming Zero Reinforcement Learning for Open Base Models in the Wild
why: The zero-RL recipe whose released GRPO-on-Qwen models are this paper's primary RLVR subjects; a central node in the post-R1 reproduction wave.

title: Absolute Zero: Reinforced Self-play Reasoning with Zero Data
why: Self-play RLVR that proposes and solves its own tasks with no curated data (same LeapLab group) — a frontier attempt to push RL beyond the base model's prior.

title: OpenAI o1 System Card
why: The model that launched large-scale RL-for-reasoning; the o1 reference point the entire RLVR-for-reasoning line (R1, Kimi-1.5, DAPO) traces back to.

title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
why: The foundational critique of ever-larger LMs (bias, environmental cost, and fluent meaning-without-understanding) — the harms-side counterweight to the scale-is-better narrative the whole atlas runs on.

title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models
why: The sweeping Stanford report that named 'foundation models' and mapped their capabilities, risks, and societal stakes; a top-tier connector hub for the alignment and eval clusters.

title: Multitask Prompted Training Enables Zero-Shot Task Generalization
why: T0 — the instruction-tuning sibling to FLAN (multitask prompted training for zero-shot generalization) that InstructGPT directly benchmarks against; a core node in the pre-RLHF instruction-following lineage.

title: RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language Models
why: The toxic-degeneration benchmark InstructGPT uses to show ~25% fewer toxic outputs when prompted to be respectful; the canonical toxicity eval anchoring the safety cluster.

title: LaMDA: Language Models for Dialog Applications
why: Google's dialog LM fine-tuned for safety and factual grounding; the contemporaneous alignment-era sibling to InstructGPT in the helpful/harmless dialog line.

title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
why: Foundational LM-agent work (sandboxed simulacra with memory, reflection, planning); one of the generative-agent designs MLAgentBench's Research Plan and Status scaffold draws on.

title: Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances
why: SayCan — grounds LM planning in robotic affordances; a canonical embodied LM-agent paper in the long-horizon-planning lineage MLAgentBench evaluates.

title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
why: Open-ended embodied LM agent in Minecraft with a growing skill library; the open-ended-decision agent design MLAgentBench cites as inspiration for its experimentation agent.

title: AutoML-GPT: Automatic Machine Learning with GPT
why: Prompts LMs with data/model cards and predicts training logs for efficient hyperparameter tuning — one of the two LM-for-AutoML systems MLAgentBench explicitly contrasts its open-ended code-executing agent against.

title: MLCopilot: Unleashing the Power of Large Language Models in Solving Machine Learning Tasks
why: Prompts LMs with past experiences to predict a final categorized hyperparameter setting; the other LM-for-AutoML baseline MLAgentBench positions its full-flexibility agent against.

title: CRUXEval: A Benchmark for Code Reasoning, Understanding and Execution
why: The code-reasoning/execution benchmark LiveCodeBench's code-execution scenario is directly built on — predict a function's output from its body — but with simpler, Code-Llama-generated functions LCB replaces with harder human-written ones.

title: StarCoder 2 and The Stack v2: The Next Generation
why: The leading open code-model family (SC2-Base 3/7/15B) LiveCodeBench evaluates; trains on competition problems, exactly the contamination source LCB's time-segmentation exposes.

title: CodeT: Code Generation with Generated Tests
why: Generates tests from problem statements to filter LLM code; the test-generation setup LiveCodeBench's test-output-prediction scenario adapts by decoupling test inputs from outputs.

title: Code Generation with AlphaCodium: From Prompt Engineering to Flow Engineering
why: The multi-stage LLM pipeline (reasoning + test generation + code + self-repair) for competition coding whose gains over naive generation motivated LiveCodeBench's holistic, multi-scenario design.

title: TACO: Topics in Algorithmic COde generation dataset
why: A large competitive-programming dataset with additional generated tests; the concurrent competition-coding benchmark LiveCodeBench differentiates from via live updates and scenarios beyond generation.

title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation Framework
why: Microsoft's multi-agent conversation framework — conversable agents that collaborate to solve tasks; a flagship agent-orchestration node cited across the agent cluster.

title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for a Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
why: Assigns role-based agents (PM, architect, engineer) to a software-dev pipeline via SOPs; a foundational multi-agent software-engineering node.

title: InterCode: Standardizing and Benchmarking Interactive Coding with Execution Feedback
why: Concurrent work to AgentBench framing interactive coding (Bash/SQL) with execution feedback — the direct analog of AgentBench's Operating System and Database environments.

title: PaLM 2 Technical Report
why: Google's frontier foundation model (powers the chat-bison AgentBench evaluates); a major model hub absent from the graph.

title: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models
why: Meta's open pre-trained transformer suite; an early open-model hub in the foundation-model lineage feeding the OSS-agent cluster.

title: Exploiting Programmatic Behavior of LLMs: Dual-Use Through Standard Security Attacks
why: The closest security sibling — treats LLMs as programmable computers and ports classical attacks (obfuscation, payload splitting, virtualization) to bypass content filters; the dual-use foundation Greshake builds the indirect-injection techniques on.

title: Discovering Language Model Behaviors with Model-Written Evaluations
why: Anthropic's model-written evaluations that surfaced RLHF sycophancy and inverse scaling — the 'models tailor answers to the user's views as reward hacking' result Greshake leverages for biased-output attacks; a foundational safety-eval hub.

title: Generative Language Models and Automated Influence Operations: Emerging Threats and Potential Mitigations
why: The disinformation/influence-operations threat model (creation vs. dissemination of propaganda) underpinning Greshake's manipulated-content and disinformation attacks; bridges LLM safety to misuse-at-scale.

title: Visual Instruction Tuning
authors: Haotian Liu, Chunyuan Li, Qingyang Wu, Yong Jae Lee
why: LLaVA — the open vision-language model Greshake uses to demonstrate injection via the visual modality (Figure 28); a major multimodal hub and the entry point for image-based prompt injection.

title: Spinning Language Models: Risks of Propaganda-As-A-Service and Countermeasures
why: Backdoors that make a model emit attacker-chosen propaganda on trigger topics — the model-poisoning analog Greshake cites as conceptually similar to indirect injection, but requiring training-time access rather than just retrieved text.

title: Prompt Injection attack against LLM-integrated Applications
why: HouYi — the first systematic prompt-injection attack against real deployed LLM-integrated apps; the production-scale successor to PromptInject's direct-injection setting.

title: "Do Anything Now": Characterizing and Evaluating In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts on Large Language Models
why: The largest in-the-wild jailbreak measurement (1,405 prompts over 131 communities) — the empirical jailbreak counterpart to PromptInject's injection framing.

title: Formalizing and Benchmarking Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses
why: The canonical formal framework + benchmark unifying prompt-injection attacks and defenses; turns PromptInject's ad-hoc attacks into a standardized evaluation.

title: StruQ: Defending Against Prompt Injection with Structured Queries
why: Implements exactly the fix PromptInject left open — separating trusted instructions from untrusted data via structured queries.

title: Benchmarking and Defending Against Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks on Large Language Models
why: BIPIA — a benchmark + defenses for the indirect-injection channel (retrieved content) PromptInject didn't cover; extends the Greshake indirect-injection line.

title: Identifying the Risks of LM Agents with an Emulated Sandbox
why: ToolEmu — the LM-emulated sandbox and 330-tool / 36-toolkit set InjecAgent is built on; lets agent risks be surfaced without real-world execution.

title: Tensor Trust: Interpretable Prompt Injection Attacks from an Online Game
why: A large prompt-injection attack/defense corpus crowdsourced from an online game; the human red-team dataset behind the direct-injection cluster InjecAgent cites.

title: AgentPoison: Red-teaming LLM Agents via Poisoning Memory or Knowledge Bases
why: Backdoors agents by poisoning their RAG memory rather than the live tool output — the memory-channel counterpart to InjecAgent's tool-output IPI.

title: Jatmo: Prompt Injection Defense by Task-Specific Finetuning
why: A white-box defense that finetunes a task-specific model with no instruction-following surface to hijack — a defense InjecAgent flags as untested on tool-integrated agents.

title: SmoothLLM: Defending Large Language Models Against Jailbreaking Attacks
why: Randomized perturb-and-aggregate defense against adversarial prompts; the robustness-by-smoothing approach in InjecAgent's evaluation-metric lineage.

title: Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms: Methods, Scaling Behaviors, and Lessons Learned
why: Anthropic's foundational manual red-teaming study (dataset, scaling behaviors); the human-red-teaming hub the instruction-hierarchy data pipeline automates.

title: Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models
why: The automated 'use an LM to attack an LM' method the instruction hierarchy uses to generate its adversarial training data; a core red-teaming node.

title: A General Language Assistant as a Laboratory for Alignment
why: Anthropic's early alignment lab and the origin of context distillation, one of the two data-generation principles behind the instruction hierarchy.

title: Ignore This Title and HackAPrompt: Exposing Systemic Vulnerabilities of LLMs through a Global Scale Prompt Hacking Competition
why: EMNLP best-paper; a global prompt-hacking competition yielding a large real-world prompt-injection/jailbreak dataset — a key injection-cluster benchmark.

title: Can LLMs Separate Instructions From Data? And What Do We Even Mean By That?
why: Formalizes and measures the instruction-vs-data separation problem that the instruction hierarchy is one answer to; the conceptual sibling to this paper.

title: Defending Against Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks With Spotlighting
why: The data-delimiter (spotlighting) defense AgentDojo evaluates; a foundational indirect-PI defense in the same cluster as StruQ and the instruction hierarchy.

title: Neural Exec: Learning (and Learning from) Execution Triggers for Prompt Injection Attacks
authors: Dario Pasquini, Martin Strohmeier, Carmela Troncoso
why: Optimization-based, learned prompt-injection triggers — exactly the adaptive-attack frontier AgentDojo's generic attacks leave open; sibling to GCG in the red-teaming cluster.

title: JailbreakBench: An Open Robustness Benchmark for Jailbreaking Large Language Models
why: Standardized, adaptive jailbreak robustness benchmark by the same ETH/Tramèr group; the jailbreak counterpart to AgentDojo's prompt-injection harness.

title: Can LLMs Follow Simple Rules?
why: RuLES — tests whether models obey simple rules under adversarial pressure (goal/rule hijacking); a rule-following precursor to AgentDojo's injection-task design.

title: Evaluating Language-Model Agents on Realistic Autonomous Tasks
why: METR's realistic autonomous-task agent eval; connects AgentDojo to the RE-Bench / HCAST / RLI autonomy-capability cluster already in the queue.

title: The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration
why: Introduces nucleus (top-p) sampling — the diverse-decoding foundation self-consistency samples its reasoning paths with.

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
why: The canonical commonsense-reasoning multiple-choice benchmark (CSQA) used across the CoT / self-consistency line.

title: Did Aristotle Use a Laptop? A Question Answering Benchmark with Implicit Reasoning Strategies
why: StrategyQA — implicit multi-hop reasoning where the decomposition is hidden; a core commonsense benchmark for chain-of-thought evals.

title: Program Induction by Rationale Generation: Learning to Solve and Explain Algebraic Word Problems
why: AQUA-RAT — algebraic word problems paired with natural-language rationales; a conceptual ancestor of chain-of-thought rationale supervision.

title: UL2: Unifying Language Learning Paradigms
why: The open 20B encoder-decoder (mixture-of-denoisers) used as the small-scale model in self-consistency's cross-scale sweep.

title: Show Your Work: Scratchpads for Intermediate Computation with Language Models
why: The 'scratchpad' precursor to chain-of-thought — letting a model write intermediate steps before answering; foundational to the reasoning-trace line.

title: Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations: What Makes In-Context Learning Work?
why: Shows in-context demonstrations work partly via format/label-space, not just correct labels — directly bears on the few-shot-vs-zero-shot reasoning question.

title: Prompt Programming for Large Language Models: Beyond the Few-Shot Paradigm
why: Early argument that careful zero-shot prompt design can rival few-shot; the prompt-programming lineage Zero-shot-CoT's single trigger sits in.

title: Solving math word problems with process- and outcome-based feedback
why: DeepMind's head-to-head of process- vs outcome-based reward on GSM8K — the conceptual bridge from GSM8K's outcome verifier to process reward models (Let's Verify, Math-Shepherd).

title: PAL: Program-aided Language Models
why: Offloads the arithmetic in a chain of thought to a Python interpreter, decoupling reasoning from computation — a canonical GSM8K-era method and a counterpart to GSM8K's calculator-annotation fix.

title: Program of Thoughts Prompting: Disentangling Computation from Reasoning for Numerical Reasoning Tasks
why: Expresses reasoning steps as executable programs so the model never does the math itself; a high-cite numerical-reasoning method reported on GSM8K alongside PAL.

title: MetaMath: Bootstrap Your Own Mathematical Questions for Large Language Models
why: Bootstraps math instruction data by rewriting GSM8K/MATH questions forward and backward; a central node in the open math-SFT lineage that DeepSeekMath and the RLVR wave build on.

title: Least-to-Most Prompting Enables Complex Reasoning in Large Language Models
why: Decomposition prompting — break a problem into easier subproblems solved in sequence — a core reasoning method evaluated on GSM8K, sibling to chain-of-thought and self-consistency already in the graph.

title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
authors: Rylan Schaeffer, Brando Miranda, Sanmi Koyejo
why: The definitive rebuttal: argues emergent 'jumps' are largely an artifact of discontinuous metrics (exact-match, multiple-choice); swap to continuous metrics and the curves smooth out. The essential companion to wei-2022-emergent-abilities.

title: Scaling Language Models: Methods, Analysis & Insights from Training Gopher
why: DeepMind's 280B Gopher — one of the five model families whose scaling curves anchor the emergence survey (TruthfulQA, MMLU); a major scaling-analysis hub.

title: Language Models (Mostly) Know What They Know
why: Anthropic's calibration study (P(True) self-evaluation) behind Figure 3D's emergent-calibration result; a foundational model-self-knowledge / honesty node.

title: Predictability and Surprise in Large Generative Models
why: Frames the unpredictability of large-model capabilities and risks ('predictability and surprise') — the conceptual backbone of the emergence and emergent-risk discussion.

title: Towards Real-World Evaluation of Agentic Work in Freelance Marketplaces
why: The direct Upwork-team predecessor that established real-job benchmarks, the initial rubric/feedback system, and the human-AI pipeline UpBench scales up.

title: Holistic Agent Leaderboard: The Missing Infrastructure for AI Agent Evaluation
why: A holistic agent-eval harness (21,000+ rollouts across 9 models x 9 benchmarks) analyzing model-scaffold-benchmark interactions; the eval-infrastructure backbone UpBench positions against.

title: Position: AI Should Not Be an Imitation Game: Centaur Evaluations
why: The position paper arguing evaluation should move from agent-alone automation to centaur (human+AI) settings — the human-augmentation framing behind UpBench's HITL design.

title: Soliciting Human-in-the-Loop User Feedback for Interactive Machine Learning Reduces User Trust and Impressions of Model Accuracy
authors: Donald Honeycutt, Mahsan Nourani, Eric Ragan
why: HCI study showing human feedback can reduce trust even as accuracy improves — a cautionary note for the supervision-quality dimension of UpBench's HITL pipeline.

title: UnifiedQA: Crossing Format Boundaries With a Single QA System
why: The T5-based unified QA model that, fine-tuned on other QA and tested under distribution shift, actually outscored 175B GPT-3 on MMLU (48.9% vs 43.9%) — directly central to MMLU's headline result.

title: Think you have Solved Question Answering? Try ARC, the AI2 Reasoning Challenge
why: The AI2 Reasoning Challenge — grade-school science QA split into Easy/Challenge; a canonical knowledge-QA benchmark MMLU positions beyond and a frequent companion eval.

title: PIQA: Reasoning about Physical Commonsense in Natural Language
why: Physical-commonsense benchmark MMLU explicitly contrasts against (its Conceptual Physics is framed as a harder PIQA); part of the saturating commonsense suite that motivated MMLU.

title: Cosmos QA: Machine Reading Comprehension with Contextual Commonsense Reasoning
why: Contextual commonsense reading-comprehension benchmark MMLU cites as a near-saturated commonsense eval — evidence that motivated moving to harder specialized knowledge.

title: RACE: Large-scale ReAding Comprehension Dataset From Examinations
why: Reading-comprehension dataset built from English exams; UnifiedQA's training source and an exam-derived precursor to MMLU's exam-style multiple-choice format.

title: Analysing Mathematical Reasoning Abilities of Neural Models
why: The DeepMind Mathematics dataset — the algorithmically-generated plug-and-chug benchmark MATH's Figure 2 contrasts against (near-solved while MATH sat at ~7%); anchors the pre-MATH math-eval baseline.

title: Generative Language Modeling for Automated Theorem Proving
why: GPT-f generative theorem proving over Metamath — the formal-proof line MATH positions natural-language problem solving against; foundational LM-for-proofs node.

title: Deep Learning for Symbolic Mathematics
why: Transformers solving symbolic integration and ODEs at >95% — the 'neural calculator' result MATH cites to argue calculation is not problem solving.

title: FrontierMath: A Benchmark for Evaluating Advanced Mathematical Reasoning in AI
why: Epoch AI research-mathematician-level benchmark built after frontier models saturated MATH; the modern successor that closes the loop on MATH's falsified scaling forecast.

title: Omni-MATH: A Universal Olympiad Level Mathematic Benchmark For Large Language Models
why: Olympiad-level math benchmark explicitly targeting MATH/GSM8K saturation; a direct contemporary successor in the math-reasoning eval line.

title: Truthful AI: Developing and governing AI that does not lie
why: The conceptual companion (shared author Owain Evans) that defines truthfulness/honesty/lying for AI — the framework TruthfulQA operationalizes; anchors the honesty cluster with MASK and CoT-monitorability.

title: Process for Adapting Language Models to Society (PALMS) with Values-Targeted Datasets
why: Values-targeted fine-tuning — the exact "fine-tune for truthfulness/values" remedy TruthfulQA recommends over scaling; an early alignment-by-curated-data node linking to ETHICS and InstructGPT.

title: Defending Against Neural Fake News
why: Grover — generating and detecting machine disinformation; the "malicious misuse" threat TruthfulQA cites, and a foundational fake-news/red-teaming node.

title: Retrieval Augmentation Reduces Hallucination in Conversation
why: Shows retrieval cuts hallucination in dialogue — the retrieval-grounding remedy for falsehoods; connects to the RAG cluster (RAG, Contriever, Lost in the Middle).

title: Understanding the Capabilities, Limitations, and Societal Impact of Large Language Models
why: The deception-and-distrust societal-impact framing TruthfulQA cites for its "accidental misuse" concern; a broad LLM-risk survey node.

title: Measuring Progress on Scalable Oversight for Large Language Models
why: The 'sandwiching' paper — GPQA's direct sibling and the experimental paradigm GPQA was built to feed; the foundational node for the scalable-oversight cluster (korbak, kale).

title: AI Safety via Debate
authors: Geoffrey Irving, Paul Christiano, Dario Amodei
why: The canonical debate proposal for scalable oversight that GPQA's nine-desiderata design is built around; a foundational oversight-method hub.

title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
why: The agenda-setting AI-safety paper (scalable oversight named here) and one of the most-cited safety references — a major missing hub linking the safety/oversight clusters.

title: Scalable agent alignment via reward modeling: a research direction
why: Recursive reward modeling — the oversight research direction GPQA cites as a target experiment; connects the oversight cluster to the RLHF/post-training lineage.

title: Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models
why: Anthropic's sycophancy study — the failure mode GPQA warns is exacerbated when annotators can't verify answers; a key safety/eval-rigor node for the oversight cluster.
